questions.q: Two knots $K_0$ and $K_1$ in $S^3$ are equivalent as knots when:
choices: They share the same image as subsets of $S^3$
There is an ambient isotopy $H: S^3 \times [0,1] \to S^3$ with $H(\cdot, 0) = \mathrm{id}$ and $H(K_0, 1) = K_1$
Their complements have the same volume
They have the same number of crossings in some planar diagram
questions.explain: Knot equivalence is ambient isotopy of $S^3$ — a continuous family of self-homeomorphisms of the ambient space starting at the identity and ending with one carrying $K_0$ onto $K_1$. Sharing an image is too strong; the other criteria are not isotopy invariants.

questions.q: How many components does a $3$-component link have, by definition?
questions.explain: An $n$-component link is an embedding of a disjoint union of $n$ copies of $S^1$, so a $3$-component link has $3$ components. A knot is a $1$-component link.

questions.q: The unknot $U$ is the equivalence class of the round circle in $S^3$. Which is true about its complement $S^3 \setminus U$?
choices: It is homeomorphic to an open solid torus $S^1 \times D^2$ (open)
It is a 3-sphere with a point removed
questions.explain: $S^3$ is the union of two solid tori glued along their boundary; the complement of the core circle of one is the open interior of the other, so $S^3 \setminus U \cong S^1 \times \mathring{D}^2$. Its fundamental group is $\mathbb{Z}$ — generated by a meridian linking the unknot once.

questions.q: Reidemeister's theorem states that two planar knot diagrams represent ambient-isotopic knots in $S^3$ if and only if:
choices: They have the same number of crossings
One can be transformed into the other by a finite sequence of R1, R2, and R3 moves (and planar isotopy)
They have the same number of components
questions.explain: The theorem gives a combinatorial criterion for 3D ambient isotopy: an isotopy of the knot in $S^3$ corresponds, on a generic projection, to a finite sequence of three local moves on the diagram (plus planar isotopy of the underlying graph).

questions.q: Which Reidemeister move changes the writhe (signed crossing count) of an oriented diagram?
choices: R2 (poking one strand under another and back)
R3 (sliding a strand across a crossing)
questions.explain: R1 introduces or removes one crossing of fixed sign, changing the writhe by $\pm 1$. R2 introduces a $+$ and a $-$ crossing simultaneously (writhe unchanged), and R3 is a slide that preserves both the number and signs of crossings.

questions.q: The standard 3-crossing diagram of the right-handed trefoil has all three crossings of sign $+1$. What is its writhe?
questions.explain: Writhe is the sum of crossing signs over the diagram. With three positive crossings the writhe is $+3$; the left-handed mirror trefoil has writhe $-3$, and the difference is what eventually distinguishes the two via the Jones polynomial.

questions.q: The Alexander polynomial $\Delta_K(t)$ is defined up to which ambiguity?
choices: A complex unit on the unit circle
It is fully canonical with no ambiguity
questions.explain: Different Seifert surfaces give Seifert matrices that differ by elementary moves and stabilizations; the resulting determinants are equal up to multiplication by units of $\mathbb{Z}[t, t^{-1}]$, which are exactly $\pm t^k$.

questions.q: Which symmetry does the Alexander polynomial satisfy?
choices: $\Delta_K(t^{-1}) = \Delta_K(t)$ up to sign and power of $t$
questions.explain: From $\Delta_K(t) = \det(V - tV^T)$ one computes $\Delta_K(t^{-1}) = \det(V - t^{-1} V^T) = (-t)^{-g} \det(tV - V^T) = (-t)^{-g} \Delta_K(t)$ where $g = \dim V$, so the two agree up to a unit in $\mathbb{Z}[t, t^{-1}]$.

questions.q: The Alexander polynomial of the trefoil knot is $\Delta(t) = t - 1 + t^{-1}$ (up to units). Compute $\Delta(1)$.
questions.explain: $\Delta(1) = 1 - 1 + 1 = 1$. In fact $|\Delta_K(1)| = 1$ for every knot (not link), because the Alexander matrix at $t=1$ presents $H_1$ of the knot complement, which is always $\mathbb{Z}$.

questions.q: The Kauffman bracket $\langle \cdot \rangle$ on unoriented diagrams is built from the local skein rule $\langle L_\times \rangle = A \langle L_0 \rangle + A^{-1} \langle L_\infty \rangle$ together with the loop value $\langle \bigcirc \sqcup L \rangle = (-A^2 - A^{-2}) \langle L \rangle$. The bracket is invariant under R2 and R3 but not under R1. To fix this, the Jones polynomial uses:
choices: Multiplication by $(-A)^{-3w(D)}$ where $w(D)$ is the writhe
A change of variable from $A$ to $q$
questions.explain: An R1 move multiplies the bracket by $-A^{\pm 3}$ and changes the writhe by $\pm 1$. Multiplying the bracket by $(-A)^{-3w(D)}$ exactly cancels this, producing an honest knot invariant. Setting $A = q^{-1/4}$ gives the Jones polynomial in $q$.

questions.q: Which is the Jones polynomial able to distinguish that the Alexander polynomial cannot?
choices: The unknot from the Hopf link
The right-handed trefoil from its mirror (left-handed) trefoil
questions.explain: $V_{3_1}(q) = -q^{-4} + q^{-3} + q^{-1}$ for the right trefoil, while the mirror has $-q^4 + q^3 + q$. They are not equal, so Jones detects chirality. The Alexander polynomial is unchanged by mirroring (up to units) and so cannot.

questions.q: The Jones polynomial of the unknot is $V_U(q) = 1$. Compute $V_U(2)$.
questions.explain: $V_U(q) \equiv 1$ as a constant polynomial, so $V_U(q) = 1$ for every value of $q$ — including $q = 2$.

questions.q: The HOMFLY polynomial $P_K(\alpha, z)$ is determined by which two pieces of data?
choices: $P(\text{unknot}) = 1$ and a single skein relation $\alpha P(L_+) - \alpha^{-1} P(L_-) = z P(L_0)$
A presentation of $\pi_1$ of the complement
The Seifert genus and the writhe
The bracket and a writhe normalization
questions.explain: HOMFLY is uniquely characterised by the value $1$ on the unknot together with the three-term skein relating the two crossings $L_\pm$ and the smoothing $L_0$ at any chosen crossing. From these alone the polynomial is computable on any diagram by skein induction.

questions.q: Under which substitution does HOMFLY specialise to the Jones polynomial $V_K(q)$?
questions.explain: Substituting $\alpha = q^{-1}$ and $z = q^{1/2} - q^{-1/2}$ into the HOMFLY skein recovers exactly the Jones skein $q^{-1} V(L_+) - q V(L_-) = (q^{1/2} - q^{-1/2}) V(L_0)$. Setting $\alpha = 1$ instead recovers Alexander.

questions.q: HOMFLY of the unknot is $P_U(\alpha, z) = 1$. What is its value at $\alpha = 2$, $z = 3$?
questions.explain: $P_U \equiv 1$ is constant, so its value is $1$ regardless of $\alpha$ and $z$. The skein relation propagates the unknot value to every other link.

questions.q: In the Reshetikhin–Turaev construction, an oriented tangle diagram is read top-to-bottom and each piece contributes a morphism in a ribbon category. A positive crossing contributes:
choices: The braiding / $R$-matrix $R: V \otimes V \to V \otimes V$
questions.explain: Crossings are precisely the braidings of the ribbon category — the $R$-matrix $R_{V,V}$ for a positive crossing of two strands labelled $V$, and $R^{-1}$ for the negative crossing. Caps and cups give evaluation and coevaluation; vertical strands give identities.

questions.q: Which ribbon category, applied to the standard $2$-dimensional representation, produces the Jones polynomial?
questions.explain: The Jones polynomial is the Reshetikhin–Turaev invariant for the quantum group $U_q(\mathfrak{sl}_2)$ acting on its $2$-dimensional standard rep. Replacing $\mathfrak{sl}_2$ by $\mathfrak{sl}_n$ on the standard $n$-dim rep gives the $\mathfrak{sl}_n$ HOMFLY specialisation.

questions.q: The Yang–Baxter equation $R_{12} R_{13} R_{23} = R_{23} R_{13} R_{12}$ on $V^{\otimes 3}$ corresponds to which Reidemeister move?
questions.explain: R3 is the slide of a strand across a crossing — three braidings on three strands rearrange in two ways, and equality of the resulting compositions is exactly the Yang–Baxter equation. R2 corresponds to $R \circ R^{-1} = \mathrm{id}$; R1 is fixed up by the ribbon twist.
